rationale: I split Materials Science by the topical research communities people actually identify with (polymers, ceramics, biomaterials, computational materials, etc.) plus cross‑cutting technique areas (characterization, surfaces) and functionally oriented material classes (electronic, optical, energy). Fourteen cards balance breadth and discoverability: common, high‑volume areas are separate while closely allied topics that are usually handled by the same researchers (e.g., ceramics & glasses; soft matter & colloids) are kept together. I deliberately did not create separate cards for device/system engineering or pure synthetic organic chemistry — those go to engineering or chemistry respectively — and I merged some borderline areas (e.g., manufacturing/scale‑up) into adjacent subspecialties rather than making tiny low‑volume cards.

subspecialties.name: Electronic Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Semiconductors, dielectrics, conductors, and device‑grade thin films
covers: Materials for transistors, 2D/III‑V/oxide semiconductors, dielectrics, transparent conductors
Examples: CMOS-compatible semiconductors, oxide electronics, doped oxides, charge‑transport layers
explicitly_excludes: Device engineering and circuit design — goes to the relevant Engineering category
Pure chemical synthesis of molecules — goes to Chemistry

subspecialties.name: Polymers
subspecialties.tagline: Synthetic and natural macromolecules, processing and structure–property links
covers: Polymer chemistry as it relates to macromolecular properties, rheology, polymer nanocomposites
Examples: block copolymers, polymer electrolytes, polymer rheology and crystallinity
explicitly_excludes: Pure organic synthesis of monomers — goes to Chemistry
Polymer device integration (e.g., electronics packaging) — goes to Engineering

subspecialties.name: Nanomaterials
subspecialties.tagline: Nanoparticles, nanowires, quantum dots and nanoscale structuring
covers: Synthesis, assembly, and properties of nanoscale materials and their size‑dependent physics
Examples: colloidal quantum dots, metal nanoparticles, nanorods, self‑assembled nanostructures
explicitly_excludes: Biological nanoparticle studies focused on biology/medicine — goes to Biomaterials or Biology
Device/system implementation — goes to Engineering

subspecialties.name: Biomaterials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials for medical, tissue and biointerfacing applications
covers: Materials interacting with biological systems: scaffolds, implants, bioactive coatings, hydrogels
Examples: tissue engineering scaffolds, drug‑delivery carriers from a materials perspective
explicitly_excludes: Clinical trials and medical practice — goes to Medicine
Molecular biology-focused studies without materials emphasis — goes to Biology

subspecialties.name: Ceramics & Glasses
subspecialties.tagline: Inorganic nonmetals: structure, processing, and mechanical/thermal behavior
covers: Traditional and advanced ceramics, glass science, high‑temperature materials, ionic conductors
Examples: advanced structural ceramics, glassy electrolytes, sintering studies
explicitly_excludes: Metallurgy and metal alloys — goes to Metals/Alloys
Full device/system integration — goes to Engineering

subspecialties.name: Composites
subspecialties.tagline: Fiber, particulate, and matrix‑reinforced materials and their interfaces
covers: Design, processing, interfacial science and mechanics of fiber‑reinforced and particle‑reinforced composites
Examples: carbon‑fiber composites, polymer matrix composites, hybrid laminate studies
explicitly_excludes: Structural engineering mechanistic design at the system level — goes to Engineering
Nanocomposites that are primarily nanoparticle physics — goes to Nanomaterials

subspecialties.name: Soft Matter
subspecialties.tagline: Colloids, gels, liquid crystals, foams and complex fluids
covers: Physics and materials science of deformable, self‑assembling and out‑of‑equilibrium soft systems
Examples: colloidal assembly, liquid crystal devices (materials focus), gel mechanics
explicitly_excludes: Biological systems where biology is primary — goes to Biology/Biomaterials
Rheology for industrial processing without materials insight — goes to Chemical/Process Engineering

subspecialties.name: 2D Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Atomically thin crystals and heterostructures (graphene, TMDCs, etc.)
covers: Synthesis, characterization and unique properties of atomically thin materials and van der Waals heterostructures
Examples: graphene, MoS2, hBN heterostructures, exfoliation and CVD growth studies
explicitly_excludes: Device physics/engineering papers focused on circuits — goes to Engineering
Bulk material studies that are unrelated to 2D phenomena — goes to the relevant class (e.g., Nanomaterials)

subspecialties.name: Optical & Photonic Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials for photonics, lasers, nonlinear optics and plasmonics
covers: Materials with tailored optical properties: photonic crystals, plasmonic metals, nonlinear crystals, gain media
Examples: metamaterials for light control, low‑loss optical dielectrics, plasmonic nanoparticles
explicitly_excludes: Optical device/system engineering (lenses, systems) — goes to Engineering
Pure theoretical optics with no materials angle — goes to Physics

subspecialties.name: Energy Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Materials for batteries, fuel cells, electrolyzers and photovoltaics
covers: Electrodes, electrolytes, catalysts, and light‑harvesting materials with emphasis on materials chemistry and physics
Examples: solid‑state electrolytes, cathode materials, perovskite and thin‑film PV materials
explicitly_excludes: System‑level device architecture, grid integration, and manufacturing of energy systems — goes to Engineering
Economic/market energy analyses — goes to Energy/Policy

subspecialties.name: Materials Mechanics
subspecialties.tagline: Deformation, fracture, fatigue and micromechanics of materials
covers: Mechanical behavior and failure mechanisms across materials classes, multiscale mechanics and constitutive modeling
Examples: fracture toughness studies, crystal plasticity, fatigue life of alloys/composites
explicitly_excludes: Structural engineering design and testing at component scale — goes to Engineering
Pure continuum mechanics without materials microstructure focus — goes to Physics/Mechanics

subspecialties.name: Surface & Interface Science
subspecialties.tagline: Thin films, coatings, adhesion, corrosion and interfacial phenomena
covers: Interfacial chemistry/physics, coating technologies, corrosion, adhesion and thin‑film phenomena
Examples: anti‑corrosion coatings, interface-driven charge transfer, atomic layer deposition studies
explicitly_excludes: Bulk material properties without interfacial emphasis — goes to the relevant bulk materials card
Industrial corrosion management practice — goes to Engineering/Industry

subspecialties.name: Materials Characterization
subspecialties.tagline: Microscopy, spectroscopy, scattering and in situ measurement techniques
covers: Development and application of analytical methods (TEM, SEM, X‑ray, AFM, neutron, ultrafast probes) for materials
Examples: new electron microscopy techniques, operando spectroscopy for materials transformations
explicitly_excludes: Technique development with applications outside materials (pure instrumentation papers) — goes to Instrumentation
Data analysis papers with no materials application — goes to Computer Science/Statistics

subspecialties.name: Computational Materials
subspecialties.tagline: Atomistic to continuum modeling, materials informatics and simulation
covers: First‑principles, molecular dynamics, phase‑field, finite element modeling and ML for materials discovery
Examples: DFT studies of defects, materials databases, ML models predicting properties
explicitly_excludes: Pure methodological ML/algorithm papers without a materials focus — goes to Computer Science
High‑level theory with no materials application — goes to Physics/Applied Math

uncovered_concerns: Manufacturing, scale‑up and industrial processing: important but often sits with Chemical/Mechanical Engineering or industry; folded into processing‑related subspecialties rather than given its own low‑volume card.

Metals and alloys: I considered a dedicated 'Metallurgy/Alloys' card but many metal studies integrate with mechanics/ceramics/composites; these papers map naturally to Materials Mechanics or Ceramics/Composites when appropriate.
Pure synthetic chemistry of small molecules and monomers: common overlap, but pure synthesis that lacks materials characterization or application should go to Chemistry rather than Materials Science.
Device/system engineering: many papers blur lines; to keep MECE, device‑level work is routed to the respective Engineering category rather than Materials Science.
